items.title: AUGERprofile
items.snippet: Profile horizon properties of auger bores collected during systematic soil survey
items.description: Combined site details of auger bores collected on both the version 1 and version 2 RUFF cards. The original codified data has been translated and compiled into meaningful textual descriptions of the various properties. The Soil Survey of England and Wales surveyed many sites during its active years of mapping, describing and sampling soils between 1939 and 1987. The standardised field description cards were designed to cater for semi-detailed, detailed and ad hoc surveys. The Auger Bore records capture the soil series and description of the soil profile examined by auger bore. Information extracted: soil series (soil characterised as a peat soil and used to determine peat presence) and peat depth (where the depth of peat is also captured on the record).
items.licenseInfo: Data originated from the Cranfield University, Land Information System LandIS® Portal as Public Data, subject to the Open Licence. See https://tinyurl.com/2r7tcrnm

items.title: Case Studies
items.snippet: Here are a selection of exemplary case studies drawn across the various sectors of interest for the portal.

headings: Flood and Water Management at Lincolnshire County Council
Soil Erosion as a Feedback System
Is sustainable agriculture spatially compatible with ‘net-zero’? An experimental analysis on the Isle of Man
Undergraduate dissertation project at the University of East Anglia
The long-term impacts of historic landfill waste
Leverage these sections to tell your initiative story. Provide key information regarding your initiatives with an opportunity to go to a specific page or app to learn more.
Soil Erosion as a Feedback System (2023)

This study, published in Soil [ https://soil.copernicus.org/articles/9/71/2023/ ], investigates soil erosion as a feedback system. Most agricultural soils erode faster than new soil is formed, which leads to soil thinning. Here, researchers used a model simulation to investigate how soil erosion and soil thinning can alter topsoil properties and change its susceptibility to erosion. The study found that soil profiles are sensitive to erosion-induced changes in the soil system, which mostly slow down soil thinning. These findings are likely to impact how we estimate soil lifespans and simulate long-term erosion dynamics.
Is sustainable agriculture spatially compatible with 'net-zero'? An experimental analysis on the Isle of Man

The majority of England's circa 20,000 historic landfills do not have modern-day leachate controls. Brand and Spencer (2023) used NATMAP Soilscapes' data in analysis to determine whether leachates are likely to reach surface or ground waters.

They found, 267 of 271 WFD Groundwater Bodies in England are beneath at least one historic landfill in areas with soils that freely drain to groundwater or with naturally wet soils that drain to groundwater. With 167 of England's 425 total catchment (groundwater) Source Protection Zones containing historic landfills with industrial, commercial, household, special, liquid sludge, and/or unknown waste in areas that have soils that freely drain to groundwater or have naturally wet soils that drain to groundwater.

items.title: Detailed Mapping Explorer
items.snippet: App showing the outline of all the detailed soil maps held in LandIS. The Detailed Mapping Explorer app is one of a series of web experience apps developed in ArcGIS Online to exemplify and demonstrate some of the key datasets provided in the LandIS Portal. The Detailed Mapping Explorer shows the range of detailed mapping conducted across England and Wales, highlighting the bounding box of each map, shown as an outline. Mapping is provided at a range of detailed scales.
items.description: The Detailed Mapping Explorer shows the range of detailed mapping conducted across England and Wales, highlighting the bounding box of each map, shown as an outline. Mapping is provided at a range of detailed scales. Contact us for more details about these maps. To use the app, you can open it directly on the Portal home page and interact with the map – zooming in and selecting features to see their properties with pop-up windows. You may also search for the app using the search bar on the home page – which will allow you to open the same app full-screen.

items.title: History of the Soils Data in LandIS
items.snippet: The long history of the soils data in the LandIS Portal
items.description: Towards a National Soil Survey Soil mapping in Britain has a long history. As long ago as 1665, the Royal Society produced a pamphlet on the “ kinds of foyls of England ”. In the 18th century reports made to the Board of Agriculture started to include county soil maps and information on top- and subsoil conditions. In 1813, Sir Humphry Davy stated “ there must be at least as many varieties of soils as there are species of rocks exposed at the surface ”. In 1815, Davy further suggested that acids were substances that contained replaceable hydrogen, that hydrogen that could be partly or totally replaced by metals, and that when acids reacted with metals they formed salts. Bases being those substances that reacted with acids to form salts and water. His statement is important for soil science because it laid the foundation for understanding acid–base reactions, central to how soils function chemically. Soil acidity is fundamentally about hydrogen ions, and Davy’s concept helps explain why hydrogen held on soil particles influences both soil pH and nutrient availability. In acidic soils, hydrogen occupies 'exchange sites' on clays and organic matter; when lime is applied, calcium replaces this hydrogen, neutralising acidity and forming salts and water. Farmers often 'lime' their fields for this reason and lime kilns dot the country, where limestone was heated to produce the lime. These chemical reactions underpin cation exchange, soil buffering, fertiliser efficiency, mineral weathering, and the management of soil fertility.

items.title: HORIZONfundamentals
items.snippet: Tabular data that can be used in conjunction with any of the NATMAP products except NATMAP soilscapes. Used in assessing or modelling the capability or vulnerability of land. HORIZON fundamentals includes detailed descriptions of the texture and structure of each soil horizon.
items.description: This product contains soil series-layer properties which describe the respective properties of four successive nominal layers within each soil topsoil, upper subsoil, lower subsoil and substrate. In addition, there are separate values for every soil series under each of four land use classes. Mean values of texture, Organic Carbon and pH are available for each soil series under arable, ley grass, permanent grass and other land use (woodland and semi-natural vegetation)

items.title: HORIZONhydraulics
items.snippet: Tabular data that can be used in conjunction with any of the NATMAP products except NATMAP soilscapes. Used in assessing or modelling the capability or vulnerability of land. HORIZON hydraulics provides a range of hydraulic properties, for example water content, for each soil series-layer (soil horizon).
items.description: This product contains soil series-layer properties which describe the respective properties of four successive nominal layers within each soil topsoil, upper subsoil, lower subsoil and substrate. In addition, there are separate values for every soil series under each of four land use classes. Calculated values for water content (theta), bulk and particle density from Pedo-transfer functions derived by Van-Genuchten are available for each soil series under arable, ley grass, permanent grass and other land use (woodland and semi-natural vegetation)

items.title: NationalSoilMap
items.snippet: The National Soil Map is a vector dataset derived from the National Soil Map for England and Wales and is the product of sixty years of soil survey work in England and Wales.
items.description: The National Soil Map (NATMAP) was published in 1983 after four years of intensive work by the field survey teams of the National Soil Resources Institute. It is the product of free survey with an average of 2 to 3 soil observations per sq km outside areas mapped during more detailed soil survey. A new improved version of the National Soil Map was introduced in 2001. It includes complete urban soil coverage and increased accuracy of line work. Every map polygon contains many attribute properties. A key attribute, SOILGUIDE_URL, contains a web link to our Soilsguide information with lots of information about the specific soil type selected. Find out more about the Soilsguide tool at https://www.landis.org.uk/soilsguide/ .

items.title: NatMap Explorer
items.snippet: This is a web map designed to support the Natmap Explorer app - one of a series of web experience apps developed in ArcGIS Online to exemplify and demonstrate some of the key datasets provided in the LandIS Portal. The Natmap Explorer showcases two key datasets in the portal, the national soil map, Natmap, and the Natmap Soilscapes data.
items.description: The Natmap Explorer showcases the national soil map, NATMAP , and the NATMAP Soilscapes data. The National Soil Map NATMAP is a vector dataset derived from the National Soil Map for England and Wales and is the product of sixty years of soil survey work in England and Wales. The National Soil Map was published in 1983 after four years of intensive work by the field survey teams of the National Soil Resources Institute (NSRI). It is the product of free survey with an average of 2 to 3 soil observations per sq km outside areas mapped during more detailed soil survey. A new improved version of the National Soil Map was introduced in 2001. It includes complete urban soil coverage and increased accuracy of line work. NATMAP Soilscapes was designed to provide a simpler and more easily understood soil map of England and Wales. Soilscapes provides a map of the soils of England and Wales and is a simplified version of the National Soil Map, produced by the National Soil Resources Institute (NSRI) of Cranfield University with support from the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). It shows, in simple terms, what the likely soil conditions are at any point in the landscape by reference to one of 27 different broad types of soil To use the app, you can open it directly on the Portal home page and interact with the map – zooming in and selecting features to see their properties with pop-up windows. You may also search for the app using the search bar on the home page – which will allow you to open the same app full-screen. Every map polygon contains many attribute properties. A key attribute, SOILGUIDE_URL, contains a web link to our Soilsguide information with lots of information about the specific soil type selected. Find out more about the Soilsguide tool at https://www.landis.org.uk/soilsguide/

items.title: NATMAP1000
items.snippet: This product is useful for anyone wanting a general understanding of the soil series within a region, for those who are not too concerned with actual line work, but only require the overall breakdown of soils within their area.
items.description: NATMAP 1000 is a series based, 1 km2 grid vector spatial data set covering the whole of England and Wales, showing the relative % of soil series flattened in a table for easy database querying. NATMAP 1000 comes from the National Soil Map. The National Soil Map was published in 1983 after four years of intensive work by the field survey teams of the National Soil Resources Institute. It is the product of "free" survey with an average of 2 to 3 soil observations per sq km outside areas mapped during more detailed soil survey. A new improved version of the National Soil Map was introduced in 2001 and the grid data files regenerated. It includes complete urban soil coverage and increased accuracy of line work.

items.title: Natmap_Soilscapes Explorer
items.snippet: App showing data from both the national soil map and the Soilscapes map, two of the most important maps held in LandIS. The Natmap Explorer app is one of a series of web experience apps developed in ArcGIS Online to exemplify and demonstrate some of the key datasets provided in the LandIS Portal. The Natmap Explorer showcases two key datasets in the portal, the national soil map, Natmap, and the Natmap Soilscapes data.

The National Soil Map NATMAP is a vector dataset derived from the National Soil Map for England and Wales and is the product of sixty years of soil survey work in England and Wales. The National Soil Map was published in 1983 after four years of intensive work by the field survey teams of the National Soil Resources Institute (NSRI). It is the product of free survey with an average of 2 to 3 soil observations per sq km outside areas mapped during more detailed soil survey. A new improved version of the National Soil Map was introduced in 2001. It includes complete urban soil coverage and increased accuracy of line work. NATMAP Soilscapes was designed to provide a simpler and more easily understood soil map of England and Wales. Soilscapes provides a map of the soils of England and Wales and is a simplified version of the National Soil Map, produced by the National Soil Resources Institute (NSRI) of Cranfield University with support from the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra). It shows, in simple terms, what the likely soil conditions are at any point in the landscape by reference to one of 27 different broad types of soil To use the app, you can open it directly on the Portal home page and interact with the map – zooming in and selecting features to see their properties with pop-up windows. items.title: NATMAPavailablewater
items.snippet: The Crop Available Water map is derived from the 1:250,000 scale National Soil Map. It shows the average crop-adjusted available water capacity of the soils across England and Wales.
items.description: The Crop Available Water map is derived from the 1:250,000 scale National Soil Map. It shows the average crop-adjusted available water capacity of the soils across England and Wales. NATMAPavailablewater is useful for agronomists and in the assessment of drought as the water available in the soil offsets the water balance between rainfall and evapotranspiration. Crop adjusted available water is used in the assessment of Agricultural Land Classification

items.title: NATMAPcarbon
items.snippet: NATMAPcarbon is a soil organic carbon map derived from the 1:250,000 scale National Soil Map and the 1:100,000 scale Corine land cover 2000 map. It provides users with a summary of the stock of carbon in the soils across England and Wales at three layer depths 0-30 cm, 30-100 cm and 100-150 cm.
items.description: NATMAPcarbon is derived from the 1:250,000 scale National Soil Map and the 1:100,000 scale Corine land cover 2000 map. It provides users with a summary of the stock of carbon in the soils across England and Wales at three layer depths 0-30 cm, 30-100 cm and 100-150 cm. NATMAPcarbon is useful for anyone wanting information on the expected carbon stock in the soils of England and Wales. This data is used as part of the Greenhouse Gas Inventory

items.title: NATMAPsoilscapes
items.snippet: NATMAP soilscapes is a simplified version of the National Soil Map
items.description: NATMAPsoilscapes was designed to provide a simpler and more easily understood soil map of England and Wales. This map of the soils of England is a simplified version of the National Soil Map and has been produced by the National Soil Resources Institute (NSRI) of Cranfield University with support from the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. It shows, in simple terms, what the likely soil conditions are at any point in the landscape by reference to one of 27 different broad types of soil. Every map polygon contains many attribute properties. A key attribute, SOILGUIDE_URL, contains a web link to our Soilsguide information with lots of information about the specific soil type selected. Find out more about the Soilsguide tool at https://www.landis.org.uk/soilsguide/

items.title: NATMAPsubsoiltexture
items.snippet: NATMAPsubsoiltexture is a subsoil texture map derived from the 1:250,000 scale National Soil Map. It provides users the range of subsoil texture classes typical at a depth of 40 cm across England and Wales.
items.description: NATMAPsubsoiltexture is derived from the 1:250,000 scale National Soil Map. It provides users the range of subsoil texture classes typical at a depth of 40 cm across England and Wales. NATMAPsubsoiltexture is useful for anyone wanting generalised and simple-to-understand information of the subsoil soil texture at a 1:250,000 scale. The subsoil texture dataset allows the user to explore the likely spatial variation of the dominant and subdominant texture classes across England and Wales. This variation can be used as input into environmental models which are influenced by the textural composition of the soil matrix. The subsoil texture at 40 cm depth is particularly useful for modelling soil influence on buried assets such as pipelines and archaeology. As this is a simplified dataset, it may or may not represent the soil texture at any given location.

items.description: NATMAPtopsoiltexture is derived from the 1:250,000 scale National Soil Map. It provides users the range of topsoil texture classes typical across England and Wales. NATMAPtopsoiltexture is useful for anyone wanting generalised and simple-to-understand information of the topsoil soil texture at a 1:250,000 scale. The topsoil texture dataset allows the user to explore the likely spatial variation of the dominant and subdominant texture classes across England and Wales. This variation can be used as input into environmental models which are influenced by the textural composition of the soil matrix. As this is a simplified dataset, it may or may not represent the soil texture at any given location.

items.title: NATMAPwrb2006
items.snippet: NATMAPwrb2006 (National Soil Map - World Reference Base 2006 Version) is derived from the National Soil Map at 1:250,000 scale map of England and Wales, showing the locations of 113 distinct soil classes wherever they occur within the countries. The dominant soil series in each soil association has been classified to show the equivalent soil class in the World Reference Base classification system of 2006 as defined in World Soil Resources reports No. 103.
items.description: NATMAPwrb2006 is useful for anyone wanting to portray the soils of England and Wales in an international soil classification. The World Reference Base of the FAO permits soils from around the world to be placed in a coherent classification NATMAP wrb is derived from the National Soil Map at 1:250,000 scale map of England and Wales, showing the locations of 113 distinct soil classes wherever they occur within the countries. The dominant soil series in each soil association has been classified to show the equivalent soil class in the World Reference Base classification system of 2006 as defined in World Soil Resources reports No. 103. Every map polygon contains many attribute properties. A key attribute, SOILGUIDE_URL, contains a web link to our Soilsguide information with lots of information about the specific soil type selected. Find out more about the Soilsguide tool at https://www.landis.org.uk/soilsguide/ .

items.title: NSI Explorer
items.snippet: App showing data from the National Soil Inventory, gridded soil monitoring datasets, for the original national survey and the follow up resurvey. The NSI Explorer app is one of a series of web experience apps developed in ArcGIS Online to exemplify and demonstrate some of the key datasets provided in the LandIS Portal. The NSI Explorer showcases a key dataset in the portal, the National Soil Inventory, NSI. The NSI site dataset is a unique collection of a vast range of detailed information. Based on a systematic grid, spaced at 5km intervals, the NSI data can be used in geostatisticical methods as well as a more general information source. Recent studies have included the use of this data in sophisticated erosion modelling. This data is essential for those trying to build up a detailed picture, for example, of chemical trends across England and Wales.
items.description: The NSI Explorer showcases the National Soil Inventory, NSI. The NSI dataset is a unique collection of a vast range of detailed information. Based on a systematic grid, spaced at 5 km intervals, the NSI data can be used in geostatisticical methods as well as a more general information source. Recent studies have included the use of this data in sophisticated erosion modelling. This data is essential for those trying to build up a detailed picture, for example, of chemical trends across England and Wales. The National Soil Inventory (NSI) profile data is a spatial, point dataset with 6127 points, located in a 5 km grid across England and Wales. NSI profile provides a very detailed description of the soil profile at the NSI point. From the texture of the soil to the mottle size and colour, many observations are recorded within this fundamental dataset. Because of the detail of this survey, it is easy to get a good understanding of the soils at the NSI points. Combining this data with chemical NSI data from the NSI topsoil datasets may reveal otherwise hidden trends in the soils of England and Wales. NSI features derives many parameters beneficial for modelling soils. These parameters, derived from readings and measurements taken at the NSI site include the depth from the surface to various layers, as well as providing information on the amount of risk that is posed to this soil by erosion and flooding. This dataset will prove important for those wanting a good understanding of the depths of the various layers in the soil profile, and those interested in mapping risks. NSI data covers England and Wales on a regular 5 km grid and provides detailed information of the soil series and a soil profile description for each intersect of the grid. It also contains samples and analysis of the soil at the location for the topsoil (0-15cm). Information extracted: soil series (for peat presence) and peat depth. Analytical information from the soil sample was used to characterise the organic carbon content for peat soils. To use the app, you can open it directly on the Portal home page and interact with the map – zooming in and selecting features to see their properties with pop-up windows. You may also search for the app using the search bar on the home page – which will allow you to open the same app full-screen.

The National Soil Inventory (NSI) profile data is a spatial, point dataset with 6127 points, located in a 5 km grid across England and Wales. From the texture of the soil to the mottle size and colour, many observations are recorded within this fundamental dataset. This dataset will prove important for those wanting a good understanding of the depths of the various layers in the soil profile, and those interested in mapping risks. items.title: NSItopsoil1
items.snippet: The initial NSI topsoil analyses. NSI topsoil1 contains a comprehensive record of many of the important elemental abundances in the soils of the UK.
items.description: NSI topsoil1 contains a comprehensive record of many of the important elemental abundances in the soils of the UK. This dataset can be used to highlight areas of element abundance, and the links between these and some soil properties. It also provides a pH reading of the soil at that point, which can be used to plot the areas more suitable for the growth of acidic soil loving plants. The NSI topsoil analyses (original) dataset is a unique collection of a vast range of detailed information. Based on a systematic grid, spaced at 5km intervals, the NSI data can be used in geostatisticical methods as well as a more general information source. Recent studies have included the use of this data in sophisticated erosion modelling. This data is essential for those trying to build up a detailed picture, for example, of chemical trends across England and Wales. NSI Summary NSI features derives many parameters beneficial for modelling soils. These parameters, derived from readings and measurements taken at the NSI site include the depth from the surface to various layers, as well as providing information on the amount of risk that is posed to this soil by erosion and flooding. This dataset will prove important for those wanting a good understanding of the depths of the various layers in the soil profile, and those interested in mapping risks. NSI data covers England and Wales on a regular 5 km grid and provides detailed information of the soil series and a soil profile description for each intersect of the grid. It also contains samples and analysis of the soil at the location for the topsoil (0-15cm). Information extracted: soil series (for peat presence) and peat depth. Analytical information from the soil sample was used to characterise the organic carbon content for peat soils.

items.title: SOILSERIES
items.snippet: Tabular data that can be used in conjunction with any of the NATMAP products except NATMAP soilscapes. SOILSERIES provides basic information on the soil series, including the modern definition of the soil.
items.description: List of the basic soil types (or SERIES) which are components of the soil associations on the National Soil Map. Every table row contains many attribute properties. A key attribute, SERIES, contains a value that can be linked to our Soilsguide information with lots of information about the specific soil type selected. Find out more about the Soilsguide tool at https://www.landis.org.uk/soilsguide/ .
